doc_title: Seven Failure Points When Engineering a Retrieval Augmented Generation System

items.evidence_span: Retrieval augmented generation encompasses using documents to augment large language models through pre-training and at inference time $[7,9,12]$.
Retrieval augmented generation encompasses using documents to augment large language models through pre-training and at inference time $[7,9,12]$.
Retrieval augmented generation encompasses using documents to augment large language models through pre-training and at inference time $[7,9,12]$.
However, challenges arise when using large language models for information extraction such as performance with long text [8].
A recent survey [19] showed that large language models are used across the RAG pipeline including retriever, data generation, rewriter, and reader.
Emerging work has looked at benchmarking RAG systems [3] but not at the failures occurring during implementation.
Software engineering research has investigated the use of RAG systems for code-related tasks [15].
Our results confirm this The unique aspects are related to the semantic and generative nature of the use of large language models including evaluating factual accuracy [16].
Prepopulate the semantic cache with frequently asked questions [1].
Research suggests fine-tuning LLMs reverses safety training [11],
Contrary to prior work with GPT-3.5 [13]
Offline evaluation techniques such as G-Evals [14]
End-to-end training enhances domain adaptation in RAG systems [18].
Emerging work has considered using LLMs for generating questions from multiple documents [4].

items.fact: The paper refers to advancements in RAG systems noted in references [8,12].
items.evidence_span: New advances for building RAG systems are constantly emerging $[8,12]$ but how they relate and perform for a specific application context has to be discovered.
See this survey for more details [19]
